one_line_takeaway: JBF is a multi-agent (Planner/Coder/Auditor) pipeline that turns jailbreak papers into standardized, runnable attack modules with a mean reproduction-fidelity deviation of only +0.26 ASR percentage points across 30 attacks, enabling a unified 30-attack x 10-model ASR benchmark that shows model robustness is highly attack-mechanism-dependent rather than uniform.
key_finding: Across the 30 reproduced attacks, mean ASR deviation from paper-reported values was +0.26 percentage points (range -16.0 to +20.0; 16 attacks with delta >= 0, 14 with delta < 0; only 2 attacks under -10 points: SCP at -11.8 and ISA at -16.0). JBF-FORGE synthesis averaged 28.2 minutes per attack (median 25.0 minutes; 82% of runs finished within 60 minutes), with planning ~4 minutes and auditing ~3.2 minutes per iteration. For the 22 attacks with runnable reference code (19 unique codebases after de-duplicating variants), integration compressed total LOC from 22,714 to 9,549 (compression ratio rho = 0.42, i.e., a 42% LOC reduction versus original repos); treating the 2,014-LOC JBF-LIB core as fixed overhead across 26 implementations, 82.5% of the integrated codebase was shared framework code and 17.5% attack-specific. A with-repo-vs-no-repo ablation on five attacks (ABJ, EquaCode, GTA, SATA-MLM, SATA-ELP) showed repositories raised mean ASR from 66.5% (no-repo) to 86.3% (with-repo), a delta of +19.8 points, versus 77.0% reported in the original papers, with gains concentrated in scaffold-heavy methods (GTA delta=+48.6, SATA-MLM delta=+34.8, SATA-ELP delta=+10.1) versus minor gains for template-style methods (EquaCode delta=+5.3, ABJ delta=+5.2). The enhanced refinement pass improved the mean gap on the six worst-performing attacks (Past-Tense, AIR, TrojFill, SCP, ISA, TRIAL) from -16.2% to -7.6%, improving 5 of 6 (ISA did not improve). In the standardized 30x10 JBF-EVAL matrix on AdvBench with a GPT-4o judge: GPT-5.1 had mean ASR 29.5% but ranged 0-94% across attacks (six attacks at 0%, but 94% on JAILCON-CIT, 90% on JAILCON-CVT, 78% on AIR); GPT-OSS-120B had the lowest mean ASR (9.13%, 15/30 attacks at 0%) yet still failed sharply on MOUSETRAP (82%) and RTS (51.1%); GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o, and Qwen3-14B had the highest mean ASRs (78.8%, 74.5%, 71.4% respectively), with GPT-3.5-Turbo's minimum ASR across all 30 attacks being 50%. Averaged over all victim-attack pairs, victim-in-the-loop search was the strongest search family (mean ASR 60.3%) and stateful selection the weakest (49.4%), though this reversed per-model (e.g., on GPT-5.1 sampling beat victim-loop 50.9% vs 26.8%; on GPT-4 victim-loop beat sampling 71.5% vs 45.0%). Formal-wrapper carriers were strongest overall (mean ASR 66.0%), followed by contextual wrappers (60.1%), with linguistic reframing weakest (39.3%), but this too was model-dependent (GPT-5.1 carrier-mean 65.2% for formal vs 26.0% for obfuscation; GPT-OSS-120B near-immune to formal carriers at 2.1%).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is JAILBREAK FOUNDRY (JBF), a system with three components: (1) JBF-LIB, a shared Python framework defining a module contract (base-class interfaces, I/O schema, typed parameter hooks) plus reusable utilities (prompt/message formatting, request/response normalization, caching, logging) for implementing jailbreak attack modules; (2) JBF-FORGE, a multi-agent planner-coder-auditor pipeline that translates a jailbreak paper (plus, when available, an official reference repository) into a runnable JBF-LIB-compatible attack module through a bounded audit loop (Algorithm 1), with an optional 'enhanced refinement pass' using a long-running Claude Code agent when matched-setting ASR undershoots the paper's reported ASR by more than 10 percentage points; and (3) JBF-EVAL, a standardized evaluation harness that fixes datasets/loaders, execution protocols across victim models, and a swappable judge to produce comparable ASR tables/heatmaps across attacks and models. The authors used this pipeline to reproduce 30 jailbreak attacks (22 with official code, 8 from paper text alone) drawn from papers reporting results on AdvBench or JailbreakBench, and then ran all 30 reproduced attacks against 10 victim models under one unified GPT-4o-judged harness to study attack-model interaction effects.
threat_model: The paper does not itself propose a novel jailbreak attack or a novel defense; it operationalizes existing published jailbreak techniques (e.g., PAIR, DeepInception, ReNeLLM, TAP, WordGame, Past-Tense, ABJ, FlipAttack, AIR, SATA, QueryAttack, Mousetrap, SCP, AIM, Response-Attack variants, PUZZLED, MAJIC, JailExpert, TRIAL, HILL, JAIL-CON variants, RTS-Attack, TrojFill, ISA, GTA, EquaCode) against black-box LLM victims, using each attack's own reported search strategy (single-pass, stochastic sampling, stateful selection, victim-in-the-loop optimization) and carrier strategy (linguistic reframing, contextual wrappers, formal wrappers, obfuscation/reconstruction, multi-strategy) as defined in the paper's own taxonomy (Section 2, detailed in Appendix A). Victim models tested include Claude-3.7-Sonnet, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, GPT-4, GPT-4o, GPT-3.5-Turbo, LLaMA3-8B-Instruct, LLaMA2-7B-Chat, Qwen3-14B, GPT-5.1, and GPT-OSS-120B. Success is measured as Attack Success Rate (ASR) under each paper's own jailbreak-success criterion, judged automatically (GPT-4o judge for standardized JBF-EVAL runs; each paper's original judge model, e.g. GPT-4, GPT-4o, or human-labeled with GPT-4o, for paper-matched reproduction in Table 1). The paper explicitly frames its own contribution as infrastructure/methodology for reproducible measurement, not as a new attack; it flags dual-use risk in its Impact Statement (reducing engineering burden to run known jailbreaks may lower the barrier to misuse) and advocates responsible deployment/release.
limitations: The paper's own stated boundaries: JBF-FORGE's main loop relies on a bounded audit process (up to T iterations) that the authors note has 'diminishing returns after a few refinement rounds' rather than guaranteed convergence to zero fidelity gap; two attacks (SCP, ISA) remained large under-reproductions even after the enhanced refinement pass, and for ISA the authors attribute the residual gap to protocol/interface details (system/message formatting, max-tokens defaults, prompt serialization) outside the refinement pass's scope rather than a fixable implementation bug. The reproduction set is restricted to papers reporting results on AdvBench or JailbreakBench specifically for comparability with JBF-EVAL, so the 30-attack sample is not representative of the full jailbreak literature. The with-repo ablation is run on only 5 of the 30 attacks, a small subsample for the reuse/fidelity claim. Standardized JBF-EVAL results use a single fixed GPT-4o judge and AdvBench, which the authors themselves note (Section 4.1) departs from each paper's own judge/dataset when those differ, meaning the cross-model matrix (Figure 4) is not directly comparable, attack-by-attack, to the paper-matched Table 1 results. The Impact Statement explicitly flags dual-use risk: the system lowers the engineering barrier to operationalizing existing jailbreak methods at scale, and the authors state they 'advocate responsible deployment and release practices' without describing what concrete access controls (if any) are placed on the released JBF-LIB/JBF-FORGE code or the 30 reproduced attack modules beyond the stated GitHub release. The paper positions its own future work (continual ingestion, defense-synthesis module, attack-defense interaction heatmaps) as not yet implemented in this version.

quantitative_results: Mean ASR deviation (ASR_generated - ASR_paper) across 30 reproduced attacks: +0.26 percentage points, range -16.0 to +20.0
16/30 attacks with delta >= 0, 14/30 with delta < 0, 2/30 with delta < -10 (SCP -11.8, ISA -16.0)
Mean synthesis time: 28.2 minutes (median 25.0 minutes); 82% of runs complete within 60 minutes
LOC compression for 22 attacks with reference code: 22,714 -> 9,549 LOC, ratio rho=0.42 (42% reduction)
Framework reuse: 82.5% shared JBF-LIB code vs 17.5% attack-specific code (across 26 implementations, 2,014-LOC core treated as fixed overhead)

With-repo vs no-repo ablation (5 attacks): mean ASR 66.5% (no-repo) -> 86.3% (with-repo), delta=+19.8, vs 77.0% original-paper mean; per-attack deltas: GTA +48.6, SATA-MLM +34.8, SATA-ELP +10.1, ABJ +5.2, EquaCode +5.3
Enhanced refinement pass on 6 worst-gap attacks: mean gap improved from -16.2% to -7.6% (5/6 attacks improved, ISA did not)

JBF-EVAL standardized 30x10 matrix: GPT-5.1 mean ASR 29.5% (range 0-94%); GPT-OSS-120B mean ASR 9.13% (15/30 attacks at 0%, but 82% on MOUSETRAP, 51.1% on RTS); GPT-3.5-Turbo mean ASR 78.8% (minimum 50% across all 30 attacks); GPT-4o mean ASR 74.5%; Qwen3-14B mean ASR 71.4%
Search-family mean ASR (all victim-attack pairs): victim-in-the-loop 60.3% (strongest), stateful selection 49.4% (weakest)
Carrier-class mean ASR (all victim-attack pairs): formal wrappers 66.0% (strongest), contextual wrappers 60.1%, linguistic reframing 39.3% (weakest)
At moderate threshold: 20/30 attacks reach >=50% ASR on at least 6/10 victims; 15/30 on at least 7/10; at stricter threshold, 13/30 attacks reach >=70% ASR on at least 6/10 victims
baselines_compared: 30 original jailbreak papers/attacks used as the fidelity baseline (ASR_paper), including PAIR, DeepInception, ReNeLLM, TAP, WordGame/WordGame+, Past-Tense, ABJ, FlipAttack, AIR, SATA-MLM, SATA-ELP, QueryAttack, Mousetrap, SCP, AIM, RA-DRI, RA-SRI, PUZZLED, MAJIC, JailExpert, TRIAL, HILL, JAIL-CON-CVT, JAIL-CON-CIT, RTS-Attack, TrojFill, ISA, GTA, EquaCode

Related (not compared empirically in this paper's own experiments, cited as prior evaluation frameworks): EasyJailbreak, TeleAI-Safety, OpenRT, HarmBench, GuidedBench, JailbreakBench evaluation harness
